The average monthly temperatures for August at Baudette and Park Rapids turned out about a degree above normal.  No daily high temperatures ever rose above 90 degrees, which was a pretty substantial difference from the hot month of July.  Precipitation amounts were highly variable, and for the most part were not widespread enough to end the drought problems.  For the month of August, National Weather Service Cooperative Observers measured 8.42 inches of rain at Argyle, 2.35 inches at Fosston, 1.31 inches at Kelliher, 1.65 inches at Roseau, 3.32 inches at Baudette, 1.06 inches at Itasca State Park, and 2.90 inches at Waskish.  Rainfall totals for May through August ranged from 12.73 inches at Argyle, 6.46 inches at Fosston, 6.76 inches at Kelliher, 4.40 inches at Roseau, 7.83 inches at Baudette, 7.98 inches at Itasca, and 8.27 inches at Waskish.  Normal amounts of rainfall for the May through August period range from 12 to 15 inches.  Lake of the Woods, Roseau, East Marshall, North and South Beltrami, Hubbard, Wadena, Pennington, Red Lake, East Polk, North and South Clearwater, Mahnomen, and East Becker counties all remained in the D3 (extreme) drought designation (per the U.S. Drought Monitor) for the entire month.  Kittson county began the month in the D3 category, but more persistent and widespread rainfall dropped it back to a D2 (severe) drought designation on the 8th.  West Marshall and West Polk also received more rainfall and fell back to a D2 category on the 15th.  East Otter Tail county began the month in a D2 designation and was upgraded to a D3 on the 15th.

The average monthly temperature for August at Moorhead was about a degree above normal.  No daily high temperatures ever rose above 90 degrees, which was a pretty substantial difference from the hot month of July.  Precipitation amounts were highly variable, but for most of the month (and continuing all the way back into May 2006) were below normal.  The NDAWN station at Sabin reported 1.89 inches of rain in May, 0.94 inches in June, 2.46 inches in July, and 1.38 inches in August, for a 4 month total of 6.67 inches.  The National Weather Service Cooperative Observer at Dalton reported 2.73 inches in May, 3.40 inches in June, 0.79 inches in July, and 5.65 inches in August, for a 4 month total of 12.57 inches.  Normal amounts of rainfall for the May through August period range from 12 to 15 inches. Clay, Wilkin, Grant and west Otter Tail counties were listed in the D2 (severe) drought category on the 8th.  More persistent and widespread rainfall ended the D2 drought designation on the 29th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud developed under a persistent wall cloud formation and was witnessed by a farmer in his field southeast of Kennedy.  The wall cloud appeared to turn to the right of its original southeast path and toward a more southerly direction at that point.  A tornadic touchdown initiated several minutes later east of Donaldson.
Several square miles of crops were shattered or flattened by large hail in eastern Davis and western Springbrook townships.

This tornado originated in Kittson county 3 miles east-northeast of Donaldson at 1602 CST.  The tornado blew over a road sign and caused additional extreme wind stress as crops were swirled by the winds.  Peak wind speeds were estimated at 80 mph.
Quarter to golfball sized hail occurred along the northwest corner of the Lower Red Lake.

The tornado touched down in a field just north of highway 11 and travelled for about 3.5 miles before it crossed into Marshall county.  Tornadic winds snapped several cottonwood trees at a homestead and swirled tree branches around the yard.  Smaller tree branches were lofted over a mile to the south by the tornado and crops were swirled by the winds.  Peak winds were estimated at 80 mph.
The hail fell near the Pennington and Red Lake county line.

A tornado touched down intermittently along a 2.5 mile track to the south, which began in Red Lake county and ended in Polk county.  The tornado swirled swaths of grain and flipped a shed door up onto the shed roof.  It also broke off numerous large tree branches in shelterbelts along its path.  Peak wind speeds were estimated at 65 mph.
The hail fell near the Red lake and Pennington county line.
A persistent wall cloud with developing funnel was observed moving to the south-southeast.  A touchdown occurred later east of Huot.
An 8 inch diameter tree was knocked down.

This tornado originated in Red Lake county 1 mile east of Huot at 1746 CST.  The tornado broke numerous large branches in various shelterbelts along its route.  Peak wind speeds were estimated at 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lack of rainfall over the summer and fall kept many counties across northwest and west central Minnesota in a D2 (severe) or D3 (extreme) drought designation (per the U.S. Drought Monitor).  Roseau, Lake of the Woods, East Marshall, Pennington, North and South Beltrami, North and South Clearwater, Hubbard, and Wadena counties were classified in the D3 category. Red Lake, East Polk, Mahnomen, and East Becker counties were grouped in the D2 category.

On tuesday morning (11/28/06), an area of surface low pressure was located west of Marshall, Minnesota.  The low moved nearly straight north through the rest of the morning, reaching the Ada, Minnesota, area by noon.  Surface temperatures were above freezing right around the low, but were in the mid to upper 20s over northwest Minnesota.  At 500 mb, a broad western trough brought nearly straight south-southwest flow over the surface low.  This resulted in warmer temperatures aloft, which caused the precipitation to fall as rain, then freeze once it hit the ground.  0.30 to 0.50 inches of ice accumulated over portions of northwest Minnesota.

An area of surface low pressure tracked from near Watertown, South Dakota, to near Herman, Minnesota, on the afternoon of the 30th.  As it did, an inverted trough extended north from the low and provided a focus for light snow.  4 to 7 inches of snow fell across portions of northeast North Dakota and extreme northwest Minnesota.  As the low shifted east, northwest winds gusted up to 40 mph and caused some near blizzard conditions in open country.  There were reports of a few cars and trucks sliding off roads due to slippery spots and limited visibility.  Several schools cancelled classes early on the 30th and also delayed starts on the 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After many areas across the northwest quarter of Minnesota had been worried about having little or no snow cover for the Christmas holiday, a late December storm system brought some ice and snow to the area.  At noon on December 30th, an area of surface low pressure had set up over southern Kansas, with an inverted trough extending north from the low (from near Sioux Falls, SD, to west of Devils Lake, ND).  A fairly strong temperature gradient set up across the inverted trough, with Minot, ND, at 16F, Devils Lake at 25F, and Crookston, MN, at 32F.  As moisture was transported into the colder air, intense snow bands set up over central North Dakota.  By mid Saturday afternoon, the inverted trough had moved into the Red River Valley.  However, temperatures still ranged in the low to mid 30s along and east of the valley.  The inverted trough remained nearly stationary through midnight, which kept temperatures above freezing over most of west central Minnesota.  Colder temperatures did filter into the northern Red River Valley and portions of northwest Minnesota closer to the Canadian border, where rain changed to freezing rain and then to snow.  By 3 AM on December 31st, the boundary pushed into central Minnesota, and most of the precipitation ended.  Areas along and north of a line from Ada, MN, to Thief River Falls, MN, to Waskish, MN, picked up about 4 inches of snow and a quarter to half an inch of ice.  Areas to the south of that line picked up very little precipitation at all.  More snow fell to the west, over portions of eastern North Dakota.

Areas along and north of a line from Ada, MN, to Thief River Falls, MN, to Waskish, MN, picked up about 4 inches of snow and a quarter to half an inch of ice.  Areas to the south of that line picked up very little precipitation at all.  More snow fell to the west, over portions of eastern North Dakota.

Freezing rain the evening of November 26th through the morning of November 27th caused light ice accumulations of a tenth of an inch or less. The freezing rain made roads and other surfaces icy, resulting in numerous traffic accidents. Fatalities resulted from two of the accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain caused light ice accumulations, but made roads and other surfaces icy, resulting in numerous accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Exteme drought conditions continued to plague all of northeastern Minnesota this December, according to the U.S. Drought Monitor.  Most of the region has been in this status since July. Lake Superior was still at its lowest level since 1926. Many area streams and rivers were at very low streamflows through December. Duluth reported its third |brown (snow depth of a trace or less) Christmas in history, and its first Christmas with a snow depth of zero.

Strong low pressure moving from the southern plains through eastern Wisconsin brought copious amounts of precipitation to northeastern Minnesota December 30th and 31st.  A warm surge of deep tropical moisture preceded the storm, and brought rainfall totals that exceeded 1 inch in places.  Freezing rain accumulated to a quarter to a half inch in some places in the interior Arrowhead region.  The Babbitt area had up to a half inch of ice accumulation, which downed trees and power lines. The rain changed over to snow during the evening of the 31st as cold air wrapped around the backside of the system. The heaviest snow occured in a 20 to 30 mile wide band from northwestern Pine County, northeast to east central St Louis County around the Babbitt area. Over 6 inches of snow occured in these areas. Specific snowfall amounts include 10 inches in Babbitt, 8.5 inches near Tamarack, and 7 to 7.5 inches at Embarrass, Floodwood, and Bruno.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was preceded by freezing rain in this New Year's Eve winter storm episode.  Ice accumulated to a quarter to a half inch thick in the Babbitt area, which downed trees and powerlines. Law enforcement reported many accidents overnight on the 30th and into the morning hours of the 31st. Freezing rain changed to heavy snow during the mid afternoon hours on the 31st as cold air moved in and a band of heavy snow set up across central St. Louis County. By the time the snow ended Sunday night, 10 inches of snow had fallen in Babbitt. Other totals include 8.5 inches at McKinley, 6.5 inches in Chisholm, 6 inches in Virginia, and 5 inches in Tower.

An upper level trough of low pressure moving across northern South Dakota and west central Minnesota along with deep and strong frontogenesis brought mostly rain along with some freezing rain and light snow to Big Stone and Traverse counties. The precipitation began as rain early in the morning on the 30th and continued through most of the day. The rain changed to freezing rain during the evening and then to snow before ending late in the evening of the 30th. Ice accumulations of over a quarter of inch occurred mainly in southwest Traverse and northwest Big Stone counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system developed in the Southern Plains on the 29th, and then moved to extreme western Minnesota on the 30th. This surface low pressure area...then proceeded into eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin by the late evening hours on the 31st. A very narrow band of heavy snow set up in a north-south orientation from east central into south central Minnesota. Some of the heaviest snow totals include: 10 inches at Mankato (Blue Earth County), 10 inches at Waconia (Carver County), 8.5 inches at Montgomery (Le Sueur County), 8.0 inches at Winnebago (Faribault County), 8.0 inches at Deephaven (Hennepin County), 7.0 inches at Zimmerman (Sherburne County), and 7.0 inches at Janesville (Waseca County). Twenty to thirty mile per hour winds produced some blowing and drifting snow during much of the afternoon hours on the 31st.

Some of the heaviest snow totals include: 10 inches at Mankato (Blue Earth County), 10 inches at Waconia (Carver County), 8.5 inches at Montgomery (Le Sueur County), 8.0 inches at Winnebago (Faribault County), 8.0 inches at Deephaven (Hennepin County), 7.0 inches at Zimmerman (Sherburne County), and 7.0 inches at Janesville (Waseca County). Twenty to thirty mile per hour winds produced some blowing and drifting snow during much of the afternoon hours on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches reported at Plymouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought affecting northeastern Minnesota from the summer through late autumn led to a continuation of D3 (extreme) drought conditions in November, according to the U.S. Drought Monitor. Most locations were well below normal for precipitation this month. Duluth recorded only 1.22 inches of precipitation, which wasd 0.90 inch below normal for the month. The drought was having adverse effects on lake levels, with Lake Superior being nearly one foot below normal in November. In fact, the last time Lake Superior was this low during November was in the mid 1920s. Many other lakes were well below their normal levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northeastern Minnesota continued to be in a D3 (extreme) drought according to the U.S. Drought Monitor. A continuing trend of below normal precipitation that plagued northeastern Minnesota in the summer and early autumn continued through October with monthly precipitation falling short of the historical normal by one to two inches. Duluth recorded 1.78 inches of precipitation for the month, which was 0.68 inches below normal. Rainfall deficits from May through October exceeded six inches in many areas.  Most of northeastern Minnesota's rivers and streams remained in the lowest 10th percentile of water flow according to the U.S. Geological Survey.
A warm frontal boundary over central and southern Minnesota was the catalyst for a rash of severe weather across a portion of the Chanhassen county warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second floor deck on an area home on Kiester's Main Street was damaged when tree limbs caused by strong winds fell over it. Several reports of large branches on area homes.
